text: During optical entry, the anterior rectus sheath is identified, followed by the visualization of the rectus muscle beneath it.
The surgeon pauses upon visualizing the rectus abdominis muscle and begins insufflation at a pressure of fifteen millimeters of mercury.
Carbon dioxide insufflation is used to dissect into the retro-rectus space, forming a cushion that facilitates safe entry into this anatomical area.
The surgeon slowly inserts the 5mm optical trocar into the retro-rectus space, advancing through the marked entry point on the skin.
The surgeon targets the layer between the subcutaneous fat and the posterior rectus sheath, preparing for further dissection in the retro-rectus space.
The surgeon performs dissection with the obturator in the retro-rectus space before removing it and inserting the scope for further visualization.
After inserting the scope, the surgeon creates sufficient space in the retro-rectus area to place a second trocar approximately six centimeters from the initial trocar site.
A needle is used to identify the track for the trocar placement in the retro-rectus space.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to further develop the retro-rectus space, preparing for the placement of a third port in the left lower quadrant.
The surgeon upsizes the initial five millimeter port to an eight millimeter robotic port, adjusting the entry point for robotic instruments.
The robotic system is docked from the patient's left side at an approximately forty-five degree angle, positioning it for the surgical procedure.
The surgeon uses a scissor in the right hand and a force bipolar in the left hand, manipulating tissues within the surgical field.
The surgeon demonstrates the dissection of stage one, focusing on the entire left retrorectus space using robotic instruments.
In the lower abdomen below the arcuate line, the surgeon carefully dissects by staying close to the rectus muscle, pulling down the transversalis fascia.
After completing the dissection of the left retrorectus space, the surgeon crosses over into the preperitoneal space, continuing the surgical procedure.
The surgeon initiates the crossover into the preperitoneal space using cold cut scissors without energy, carefully manipulating the tissue without cautery.
The surgeon confirms the absence of bowel in the surgical field and proceeds to use an energy device to expedite the dissection process.
The surgeon approaches the area of the hernia sac, carefully dissecting and removing the posterior sheath to avoid damaging surrounding structures.
The surgeon observes the translucent appearance of the peritoneum, indicating its thinness and proximity to the surgical field.
The surgeon navigates through the preperitoneal space, maintaining a plane between the abundant preperitoneal fat and the anterior fascia.
The surgeon has dissected across to the area of the right rectus muscle, but holds off on crossing over until the preperitoneal dissection is fully completed.
The surgeon continues to open the posterior sheath approximately one centimeter away from the linea alba, carefully advancing the dissection in the area near the hernia sac.
The surgeon identifies the location of the hernia and proceeds with extra caution, ensuring careful manipulation of the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon observes the thin peritoneum with an absence of preperitoneal fat, highlighting the anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon sizes the posterior sheath along its entire length, progressing down to below the arcuate line, while maintaining careful dissection.
Below the arcuate line, the surgeon searches for the transverse salus fascia, navigating through the exposed surgical field.
The transverse salus fascia is visible, behaving similarly to the posterior sheath in its appearance and structure.
The surgeon identifies and cuts the left transverse salus fascia, a visible structure in the surgical field.
The surgeon cuts the left transverse salus fascia, revealing the preperitoneal space beneath.
The surgeon searches for the right transverse salus fascia, visually identifying the structure within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues to focus on the left transverse salus fascia, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses scissors to cut the left transverse salus fascia, entering the preperitoneal space and peeling down the preperitoneal fat, revealing the right transverse salus fascia.
The surgeon opens the right transverse salus fascia, revealing the underlying rectus muscle.
The surgeon continues to dissect the preperitoneal tissue, carefully taking down the hernia after crossing over the transverse salus fascia.
The surgeon returns to the hernia area, focusing on the preperitoneal space where previous dissection occurred.
The surgeon maintains dissection anterior to the peritoneum and hernia sac, ensuring these structures remain undisturbed during the procedure.
The surgeon splits the diastatic linea alba, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon exposes a clear view of the arcuate line, a visible anatomical landmark in the abdominal wall.
The surgeon plans to continue dissecting the posterior sheath later in the procedure.
The surgeon focuses on taking down the hernia sac, working anterior to the peritoneum and hernia area.
The surgeon encounters a small piece of proline mesh in the surgical area, causing the hernia sac to appear sticky during the takedown process.
The surgeon uses scissors to carefully dissect the peritoneum, ensuring there is no bowel on the opposite side before proceeding.
The surgeon carefully uses cautery to dissect through the peritoneum, ensuring visibility to avoid bleeding during the procedure.
The surgeon separates layers of the pseudosac or the diastatic linea alba, carefully distinguishing between the anatomical structures during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers outside the peritoneal cavity, avoiding entry into the cavity during the hernia sac takedown.
The surgeon occasionally enters the subcutaneous space to preserve the integrity of the posterior layer with the peritoneum.
The surgeon encounters difficulty while performing the takedown of the hernia sac, indicating a challenging dissection process.
The surgeon incises the contralateral posterior sheath approximately one centimeter away from the linea alba, near the arcuate line.
The surgeon prepares to make an incision near the umbilicus, instructing the assistant to insert a finger or instrument into the umbilicus to avoid cutting across the umbilical stalk.
The assistant wiggles his finger within the stalk of the umbilicus, providing tactile feedback to avoid accidental cutting during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the division of the umbilical stalk from the hernia sac, separating these structures visibly.
The surgeon continues to dissect and remove the hernia sac, following the incision near the arcuate line and contralateral posterior sheath.
The surgeon breaches the peritoneum, visibly entering the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon continues to take down the hernia sac within the peritoneal cavity, where the boundaries of the peritoneal sac are visible.
The surgeon has successfully taken down most of the hernia sac, with visible separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon dissects on the opposite side of the neck of the hernia sac, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon completes the dissection and removal of the hernia sac, marking the end of stage two. The next step involves entering the contralateral retroactive space.
The surgeon, using their right hand, inserts a grasper to open the retroactive space, moving from left to right.
The surgeon inserts a scissor into the retroactive space and makes a cut.
The surgeon accesses the upper incisional hernia, preparing to address the hernia defect.
The surgeon enters the peritoneal cavity and carefully dissects omental adhesions within the hernia sac.
A small tear in the peritoneum is visible, which will be closed later using a V-lock suture.
The surgeon dissects the preperitoneal space past the linea alba and proceeds to open the posterior sheath.
The surgeon uses a left-hand grasper to check the position of the linea alba, ensuring that the scissors do not accidentally cut across it, which could create an incisional hernia.
The surgeon opens the posterior sheath well above the site of the incisional hernia, ensuring adequate space for mesh overlap.
The surgeon performs further dissection on the ipsilateral side, cutting more of the ipsilateral posterior sheath.
The surgeon adjusts the flex joint to increase the range of motion in the right hand, facilitating further surgical maneuvers.
The flex joint is visibly adjusted, first with arm four and then with arm three, to enhance the range of motion during the procedure.
The surgeon adjusts the flex joint to unstack the instruments, enhancing the range of motion at the extremes of the surgical field.
The surgeon's right hand now has increased range of motion, allowing unrestricted access to the left upper quadrant.
The surgeon extends the incision along the posterior sheath up to the xiphoid process, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon develops the right retrorectal space, expanding the area for surgical access and manipulation.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers instruments to stay close to the posterior sheath, maintaining proximity to this anatomical structure during the procedure.
The surgeon divides small neurovascular bundles, likely within the developed right retrorectal space, using precise surgical techniques.
The surgeon carefully preserves the larger neurovascular bundles, ensuring they remain intact during the procedure.
The surgeon completes the retro-rectus dissection, marking the end of the three stages of dissection. The surgical field is prepared for closure of the anterior fascia and posterior defects.
The surgeon examines the posterior defect, assessing its closability without the need for component separation.
The surgeon begins the closure of the anterior fascia, focusing on bringing the tissue edges together.
The surgeon closes the defect using an OV-Lock suture along the linea alba, ensuring the closure of the anterior fascia.
The surgeon carefully places sutures along the ipsilateral linea alba, avoiding tightening them immediately due to the difficulty in visualizing this anatomical structure.
The surgeon uses a suture to grasp a portion of the umbilical stock, preparing for closure.
The surgeon uses the entire length of the suture to close the defect and prepares to start with another eighteen-inch suture.
The surgeon uses a suture to grasp the subcutaneous tissue or pseudosac, aiming to reduce the dead space and decrease the size of the seroma.
The surgeon places two stitches in the small defect and begins the process of closing it.
The surgeon pulls the suture until tension is felt, then stops and prepares to move on to the next suture.
The surgeon places multiple sutures along the length of the incision, redistributing tension to achieve closure similar to tightening a corset.
The surgeon sequentially tightens multiple sutures placed along the surgical site, gradually increasing tension to achieve closure.
The surgeon begins tightening the sutures, increasing the length of suture near the needle to facilitate additional throws.
The surgeon begins using a third, eighteen-inch suture to continue closing the defect.
The surgeon re-approximates the linea alba, aligning the midline connective tissue rather than merely closing individual defects.
The surgeon re-approximates the diastasis, bringing the tissue together up to approximately four centimeters below the xiphoid process.
The surgeon sequentially tightens the sutures to re-approximate the defects along the linea alba, visibly bringing the separated tissue edges closer together.
The surgeon gains length on the suture and places additional throws to secure the closure of the diastasis defect.
The surgeon completes the closure of the defect and begins running the suture backwards, adding a few additional throws to secure the closure.
The surgeon runs the suture back along the initial suture line, locking it in place and providing additional reinforcement to the closed defect.
The surgeon positions the mesh to extend approximately three to four centimeters beyond the diastasis closure, ensuring adequate coverage over the repaired area.
The surgeon closes the posterior peritoneal defect using a running suture technique, progressively securing the tissue edges together.
The surgeon performs a partial transversus abdominis release (TAR) starting at the arcuate line, dividing sections of the posterior sheath to facilitate medialization of its edge.
The surgeon measures the floor space of the surgical area to determine the size of the mesh needed for coverage.
The surgeon considers using a self-gripping lightweight mesh to cover the ceiling area for smaller hernias.
The surgeon places a medium-weight macroporous polypropylene mesh on the floor of the surgical field, preparing to cover the area affected by the incisional hernia.
The surgeon measures the space intended for mesh placement, noting dimensions of seventeen centimeters wide and thirty-two centimeters long.

The surgeon cuts a 45 by 30 centimeter piece of parietine mesh to dimensions of 17 centimeters wide and 32 centimeters long. The mesh is inserted through an eight millimeter port, with larger pieces requiring removal of the port seal for insertion. The mesh is not secured as it will be sandwiched between tissue layers.
